Identification.LongTitle: An Act to amend the National Defence Act and to make related and consequential amendments to other Acts
Identification.ShortTitle: An Act to amend the National Defence Act and to make related and consequential amendments to other Acts
Identification.RunningHead: An Act to amend the National Defence Act and to make related and consequential amendments to other Acts

Provision.Text: This enactment amends provisions of the National Defence Act governing the military justice system.

It adds a new Division, entitled “Declaration of Victims Rights”, to the Code of Service Discipline, that specifies that victims of service offences have a right to information, protection, participation and restitution in respect of service offences. It adds or amends several definitions, including “victim” and “military justice system participant”, and specifies who may act on a victim’s behalf for the purposes of that Division.
It amends Part III of that Act to, among other things,

Provision.Label: (a)
Provision.Text: specify the purpose of the Code of Service Discipline and the fundamental purpose of imposing sanctions at summary hearings;

Provision.Label: (b)
Provision.Text: protect the privacy and security of victims and witnesses in proceedings involving certain sexual offences;

Provision.Label: (c)
Provision.Text: specify factors that a military judge is to take into consideration when determining whether to make an exclusion order;

Provision.Label: (d)
Provision.Text: make testimonial aids more accessible to vulnerable witnesses;

Provision.Label: (e)
Provision.Text: allow witnesses to testify using a pseudonym in appropriate cases;

Provision.Label: (f)
Provision.Text: on application, make publication bans for victims under the age of 18 mandatory;

Provision.Label: (g)
Provision.Text: in certain circumstances, require a military judge to inquire of the prosecutor if reasonable steps have been taken to inform the victims of any plea agreement entered into by the accused and the prosecutor;

Provision.Label: (h)
Provision.Text: provide that the acknowledgment of the harm done to the victims and to the community is a sentencing objective;

Provision.Label: (i)
Provision.Text: provide for different ways of presenting victim impact statements;

Provision.Label: (j)
Provision.Text: allow for military impact statements and community impact statements to be considered for all service offences;

Provision.Label: (k)
Provision.Text: provide, as a principle of sentencing, that particular attention should be given to the circumstances of Aboriginal offenders;

Provision.Label: (l)
Provision.Text: provide for the creation, in regulations, of service infractions that can be dealt with by summary hearing;

Provision.Label: (m)
Provision.Text: provide for a scale of sanctions in respect of service infractions and for the principles applicable to those sanctions;

Provision.Label: (n)
Provision.Text: provide for a six-month limitation period in respect of summary hearings; and

Provision.Label: (o)
Provision.Text: provide superior commanders, commanding officers and delegated officers with jurisdiction to conduct a summary hearing in respect of a person charged with having committed a service infraction if the person is at least one rank below the officer conducting the summary hearing.

Finally, the enactment makes related and consequential amendments to certain Acts. Most notably, it amends the Criminal Code to include military justice system participants in the class of persons against whom offences relating to intimidation of a justice system participant can be committed.
Her Majesty, by and with the advice and consent of the Senate and House of Commons of Canada, enacts as follows:

Section.Text: The heading before section 2 of the French version of the National Defence Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: The definitions service tribunal and summary trial in subsection 2(1) of the Act are repealed.
The definition infraction d’ordre militaire in subsection 2(1) of the French version of the Act is replaced by the following:

Definition.Text: infraction d’ordre militaire Infraction — à la présente loi, au Code criminel ou à une autre loi fédérale — commise par un justiciable du code de discipline militaire. (service offence)

Subsection.Text: Subsection 2(1) of the Act is amended by adding the following in alphabetical order:

Definition.Text: military justice means all aspects of the application of the Code of Service Discipline; (justice militaire)
military justice system participant means a person who plays a role in the administration of military justice, including

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the Minister,

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the Judge Advocate General,

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: an officer and non-commissioned member who acts under the supervision of the Judge Advocate General,

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: a prosecutor and counsel for an accused person,

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: a military judge,

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: a superior commander, a commanding officer and a delegated officer, as defined in section 162.3,

Paragraph.Label: (g)
Paragraph.Text: a custody review officer, as defined in section 153,

Paragraph.Label: (h)
Paragraph.Text: a member of a panel of a General Court Martial and an officer and non-commissioned member who has been appointed to be a member of a such a panel,

Paragraph.Label: (i)
Paragraph.Text: an officer and non-commissioned member who is appointed by a commanding officer for the purpose of supporting a court martial,

Paragraph.Label: (j)
Paragraph.Text: an officer and non-commissioned member who is authorized to lay or refer a charge,

Paragraph.Label: (k)
Paragraph.Text: a prospective witness, a witness who has been summoned to appear and a witness who has testified,

Paragraph.Label: (l)
Paragraph.Text: an officer and non-commissioned member referred to in paragraph (g) of the definition peace officer in section 2 of the Criminal Code,

Paragraph.Label: (m)
Paragraph.Text: a commanding officer of a service prison or detention barrack and a person who acts under the supervision of such a commanding officer, and

Paragraph.Label: (n)
Paragraph.Text: a person who acts under the supervision of the Chief Military Judge or the Court Martial Administrator; (personne associée au système de justice militaire)

Definition.Text: service infraction means a service infraction created by regulations made by the Governor in Council; (manquement d’ordre militaire)
summary hearing means a hearing conducted under section 163; (audience sommaire)

victim means an individual against whom a service offence has been committed, or is alleged to have been committed, who has suffered, or is alleged to have suffered, physical or emotional harm, property damage or economic loss as a result of the commission or alleged commission of the offence and includes, for the purposes of Division 1.1 of Part III, an individual who has suffered physical or emotional harm, property damage or economic loss as a result of the commission of a service offence against any person. (victime)

Subsection.Text: Section 2 of the Act is amended by adding the following after subsection (1):
Any of the following individuals may exercise a victim’s rights under Division 1.1 of Part III:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: if the victim is dead or is incapable, other than for operational reasons, of acting on their own behalf,

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: the victim’s spouse or the individual who was at the time of the victim’s death their spouse,

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: the individual who is, or was at the time of the victim’s death, cohabiting with them in a conjugal relationship, having so cohabited for a period of at least one year,

Subparagraph.Label: (iii)
Subparagraph.Text: a relative or dependant of the victim,

Subparagraph.Label: (iv)
Subparagraph.Text: an individual who has in law or fact custody of, or is responsible for the care or support of, the victim, and

Subparagraph.Label: (v)
Subparagraph.Text: an individual who has in law or fact custody of, or is responsible for the care or support of, a dependant of the victim; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: if, for operational reasons, the victim is unable to act on their own behalf and has requested the appointment of a member of the Canadian Forces to act on their behalf, a member appointed by the Chief of the Defence Staff or any officer authorized by the Chief of Defence Staff.

Subsection.Text: An individual is not a victim in relation to a service offence, or entitled to exercise a victim’s rights under Division 1.1 of Part III, if the individual is charged with the offence, found guilty of the offence or found unfit to stand trial or not responsible on account of mental disorder in respect of the offence.

MarginalNote.HistoricalNote: R.S., c. 31 (1st Supp.), s. 60 (Sch. I, s. 13)

Section.Text: Paragraph 30(4)(a) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: an officer or non-commissioned member has been released from the Canadian Forces or transferred from one component to another by reason of a sentence of dismissal or a finding of guilty by a court martial or any civil court, and

Section.Text: The Act is amended by adding the following after the heading of Part III:

Subsection.Text: The purpose of the Code of Service Discipline is to maintain the discipline, efficiency and morale of the Canadian Forces.

For greater certainty, the behaviour of persons who are subject to the Code of Service Discipline relates to the discipline, efficiency and morale of the Canadian Forces even when those persons are not on duty, in uniform or on a defence establishment.
Paragraphs 66(1)(a) and (b) of the Act are replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: has been found not guilty by a court martial, civil court or court of a foreign state on a charge of having committed that offence; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: has been found guilty by a court martial, civil court or court of a foreign state on a charge of having committed that offence.

Subsection.Text: Subsections 66(2) and (3) of the Act are replaced by the following:
Nothing in subsection (1) affects the validity of a new trial held under section 249 or a new trial directed by a court having jurisdiction to do so.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Effect of other offences admitted at previous trial
Subsection.Text: A person who, under section 194, has been sentenced in respect of a service offence admitted by that person may not be tried by a court martial or civil court in respect of that offence.

Section.Text: The portion of section 70 of the Act before paragraph (a) is replaced by the following:

Section.MarginalNote: Offences not triable by courts martial
Section.Text: A court martial does not have jurisdiction to try any person charged with any of the following offences committed in Canada:
The Act is amended by adding the following after section 71:
For the purposes of this Division, military justice system means

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the investigation of service offences, the processes for the laying and referral of charges of service offences and their prosecution;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the process for the carrying out of punishments in relation to service offences, except in respect of any service prisoners and service convicts who have been committed to a penitentiary or civil prison; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the proceedings of a court martial or a Review Board, as defined in section 197, in respect of an accused person who is found unfit to stand trial or not responsible on account of mental disorder.

Section.Text: Every victim has the right, on request, to information about

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the military justice system and the role of victims in it;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the services and programs available to them as a victim; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: their right to file a complaint for an infringement or denial of any of their rights under this Division.

Section.Text: Every victim has the right, on request, to information about

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the status and outcome of the investigation into the service offence; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the location of proceedings in relation to the offence, when they will take place and their progress and outcome.

Subsection.Text: Every victim has the right, on request, to information about

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the offender while they are in a service prison or detention barrack;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the release of the offender from a service prison or detention barrack;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: hearings held for the purpose of making dispositions under any of sections 201, 202 and 202.16 and the dispositions made at those hearings; and

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: hearings held by a Review Board under section 202.25 and the dispositions made at those hearings.

Subsection.Text: Information may be disclosed for the purposes of paragraphs (1)(a) and (b) subject to and in accordance with regulations made by the Governor in Council.

Section.Text: Every victim has the right to have their security considered by the appropriate authorities in the military justice system.
Every victim has the right to have reasonable and necessary measures taken by the appropriate authorities in the military justice system to protect the victim from intimidation and retaliation.
Every victim has the right to have their privacy considered by the appropriate authorities in the military justice system.
Every victim has the right to request that their identity be protected if they are a complainant in respect of the service offence or a witness in proceedings relating to the service offence.
Every victim has the right to request testimonial aids when appearing as a witness in proceedings relating to the service offence.

Every victim has the right to convey their views about decisions to be made by appropriate authorities in the military justice system that affect the victim’s rights under this Division and to have those views considered.
Every victim has the right to present a victim impact statement to the appropriate authorities in the military justice system and to have it considered.
Every victim has the right to have the court martial consider making a restitution order against the offender.
Every victim in whose favour a restitution order is made has the right, if they are not paid, to have the order entered as a civil court judgment that is enforceable against the offender.

Subsection.Text: This Division applies in respect of a victim of a service offence in their interactions with the military justice system

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: while the offence is being investigated or prosecuted or the charge is being referred;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: while the offender is serving a punishment in relation to the offence, unless the offender is a service prisoner or service convict who has been committed to a penitentiary or civil prison; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: while the accused person is, in relation to the offence, under the jurisdiction of a court martial or a Review Board, as defined in section 197, if they are found unfit to stand trial or not responsible on account of mental disorder.

Subsection.Text: For the purpose of subsection (1), if an offence is reported to the appropriate authorities in the military justice system, the investigation of the offence is deemed to begin at the time of the reporting.
The rights of victims under this Division are to be exercised through the mechanisms provided by law.
A victim is entitled to exercise their rights under this Division only if

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: they are present in Canada; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: they are a Canadian citizen or a permanent resident within the meaning of subsection 2(1) of the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act.

Subsection.Text: Unless he or she is of the opinion that it is not possible to do so for operational reasons, a commanding officer shall, at the request of the victim, appoint an officer or non-commissioned member, who satisfies the conditions established in regulations made by the Governor in Council, to be a liaison officer to assist the victim as provided for in subsection (3). The commanding officer shall, to the extent possible, appoint the officer or non-commissioned member who has been requested by the victim to be their liaison officer.

In the event of the absence or incapacity of the victim’s liaison officer, a commanding officer shall appoint another officer or non-commissioned member to replace the liaison officer during that absence or incapacity, unless it is not possible to do so for operational reasons.
Assistance by a victim’s liaison officer consists of

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: explaining to the victim the manner in which service offences are charged, dealt with and tried under the Code of Service Discipline; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: obtaining and transmitting to the victim information relating to a service offence that the victim has requested and to which the victim has a right under this Division.

Section.Text: This Division is to be construed and applied in a manner that is reasonable in the circumstances and in a manner that is not likely to

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: interfere with the proper administration of military justice, including

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: by causing interference with investigative discretion or by causing excessive delay in, compromising or hindering the investigation of any service offence,

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: by causing interference with charge laying discretion in respect of any service offence, or by causing excessive delay in, compromising or hindering the laying or referral of a charge in respect of any service offence, and

Subparagraph.Label: (iii)
Subparagraph.Text: by causing interference with prosecutorial discretion or by causing excessive delay in, compromising or hindering the prosecution of any service offence;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: interfere with ministerial discretion in respect of any service offence;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: interfere with the discretion that may be exercised by any person or body authorized to release an accused person or offender into the community;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: endanger the life or safety of any individual; or

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: cause injury to international relations, national defence or national security.

Section.MarginalNote: Interpretation of other Acts, regulations, etc.
Section.Text: To the extent that it is possible to do so, every Act of Parliament enacted — and every order, rule or regulation made under such an Act — before, on or after the day on which this Division comes into force is to be construed and applied in a manner that is compatible with the rights provided for under this Division.

Subsection.Text: If, after the application of sections 71.17 and 71.18, there is any inconsistency between any provision of this Division and any provision of any Act, order, rule or regulation referred to in section 71.18, the provision of this Division prevails to the extent of the inconsistency.

Subsection (1) does not apply in respect of the Canadian Bill of Rights, the Canadian Human Rights Act, the Official Languages Act, the Access to Information Act, the Privacy Act and the Canadian Victims Bill of Rights and in respect of any orders, rules and regulations made under any of those Acts.

Section.Text: No adverse inference is to be drawn against a person who is charged with a service offence from the fact that an individual has been identified as a victim in relation to the service offence.
Nothing in this Division is to be construed so as to permit any individual to

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: enter Canada or remain in Canada beyond the end of the period for which they are authorized to so remain;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: delay any removal proceedings or prevent the enforcement of any removal order; or

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: delay any extradition proceedings or prevent the extradition of any person to or from Canada.

Subsection.Text: Every victim who is of the opinion that any of their rights under this Division have been infringed or denied by an authority within the military justice system has the right to file a complaint in accordance with regulations made by the Governor in Council.
Regulations made by the Governor in Council may, among other things, provide for

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the review of complaints involving alleged infringements or denials of rights under this Division;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the power to make recommendations to remedy such infringements and denials; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the obligation to notify victims of the result of those reviews and of any recommendations that were made.

Section.Text: Nothing in this Division is to be construed as granting to, or removing from, any victim, any individual acting on behalf of a victim or any victim’s liaison officer the status of a party, intervenor or observer in any proceedings.

No cause of action or right to damages arises from an infringement or denial of a right under this Division. For greater certainty, nothing in this section is to be construed as affecting any other cause of action or right to damages.
No appeal lies from any decision or order solely on the grounds that a right under this Division has been infringed or denied.
Subsection 118(1) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: For the purposes of this section and section 119, tribunal includes a court martial, a military judge, an officer conducting a summary hearing, the Grievances Committee, the Military Judges Inquiry Committee, the Military Police Complaints Commission, a board of inquiry, a commissioner taking evidence under this Act and any inquiry committee established under regulations.

Section.Text: Section 118.1 of the Act is replaced by the following:

Every person who, being duly summoned or ordered to appear before a court martial or a military judge, as an accused, or before an officer conducting a summary hearing, as a person charged with having committed a service infraction, fails, without lawful excuse, the proof of which lies on the person, to appear as summoned or ordered, or to remain in attendance, is guilty of an offence and on conviction is liable to imprisonment for less than two years or to less punishment.

Subsection.Text: The portion of subsection 130(1) of the French version of the Act after paragraph (b) is replaced by the following:

ContinuedSectionSubsection.Text: Quiconque en est condamné encourt la peine prévue au paragraphe (2).

Subsection.Text: The portion of subsection 130(2) of the Act before paragraph (a) is replaced by the following:
Subject to subsection (3), if a court martial convicts a person under subsection (1), it shall,

Section.Text: Subsections 137(2) and (3) of the Act are repealed.
Section 138 of the Act is replaced by the following:
A court martial may, instead of making a finding of not guilty, make a special finding of guilty if it concludes that

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the facts proved in respect of an offence being tried by it differ materially from the facts alleged in the statement of particulars but are sufficient to establish the commission of the offence charged; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the difference between the facts proved and the facts alleged in the statement of particulars has not prejudiced the accused person in their defence.

ContinuedSectionSubsection.Text: If the court martial makes a special finding of guilty, it shall state the differences between the facts proved and the facts alleged in the statement of particulars.

Section.Text: Subsection 141(1) of the English version of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: If a court martial imposes a punishment of dismissal with disgrace from Her Majesty’s service on an officer or non-commissioned member, it may, in addition, despite any other provision of this Division, impose a punishment of imprisonment for less than two years.

Section.Text: Subsection 145(3) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: The terms of payment of a fine may be varied by the military judge who imposed the fine or a military judge designated by the Chief Military Judge.

Section.Text: Section 147 of the Act is repealed.
The Act is amended by adding the following after section 147.5:

Subsection.Text: An information may, in accordance with regulations made by the Governor in Council, be laid before a military judge by or on behalf of any victim who fears on reasonable grounds that a person who is subject to the Code of Service Discipline will cause physical or emotional harm to the victim, to the victim’s spouse, to a person who is cohabiting with the victim in a conjugal relationship, having so cohabited for a period of at least one year, or to the victim’s child or will cause damage to the victim’s property.
The military judge who receives the information shall cause the parties to appear before the military judge, either in person or otherwise.

The military judge may, if satisfied by the evidence that there are reasonable grounds for the victim’s fears, order that the person who is subject to the Code of Service Discipline and who is referred to in the information

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: abstain from communicating, directly or indirectly, with any of the following individuals who are specified in the order:

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: the victim,

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: the victim’s spouse,

Subparagraph.Label: (iii)
Subparagraph.Text: a person who is cohabiting with the victim in a conjugal relationship, having so cohabited for a period of at least one year,

Subparagraph.Label: (iv)
Subparagraph.Text: the victim’s child;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: refrain from going to any place specified in the order; or

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: comply with any other condition specified in the order that the military judge considers necessary.

Subsection.Text: If, for operational reasons, no military judge is available, the information may be laid before a commanding officer and, if it is so laid, that commanding officer has all the powers of a military judge that are set out in subsection (3).

Every decision of a commanding officer to make or not make an order under subsection (3) must be reviewed as soon as feasible by a military judge. The military judge may, at the conclusion of the review, amend any order that was made and, if none was made, make any order under that subsection.
The portion of subsection 157(1) of the Act before paragraph (a) is replaced by the following:

Subject to subsection (2), every commanding officer, and every officer to whom a commanding officer has, under section 162.94, delegated the power to conduct a summary hearing, may by a warrant under his or her hand authorize any person to arrest any person subject to the Code of Service Discipline who
Paragraphs 157(1)(a) to (c) of the English version of the Act are replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: has committed a service offence;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: is believed on reasonable grounds to have committed a service offence; or

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: is charged under this Act with having committed a service offence.

Subsection.Text: The portion of subsection 157(1) of the English version of the Act after paragraph (c) is repealed.
Section 157 of the Act is amended by adding the following after subsection (2):

An officer authorized to issue a warrant under this section shall not issue a warrant for the arrest of any person who is a member of, serving with, or attached or seconded to the same unit of the Canadian Forces as the officer.
Paragraph 158(1)(d) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: the need to ensure that the person under arrest will appear before a court martial or civil court to be dealt with according to law;

Subsection.Text: Paragraph 158(1)(f) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: the necessity to ensure the safety and security of the person under arrest, any victim of the offence, or any other person.

Subsection.Text: Section 158.6 of the Act is amended by adding the following after subsection (1):

Subsection.MarginalNote: Consideration of victim’s safety and security
Subsection.Text: If the custody review officer directs that the person be released, with or without conditions, the custody review officer shall include in the direction a statement that he or she has considered the safety and security of every victim of the alleged offence.
The custody review officer shall, on request by a victim of the alleged offence, cause a copy of the direction to be given to the victim.
Subsection 158.6(3) of the Act is replaced by the following:

After giving a representative of the Canadian Forces and the released person an opportunity to be heard, the officer conducting the review may make any direction that a custody review officer may make under subsection (1). If he or she makes a direction, subsections (1.1) and (1.2) apply with any necessary modifications.

Section.Text: The Act is amended by adding the following after section 158.6:

If a custody review officer directs that a person be retained in custody, the custody review officer may also direct that the person abstain from communicating, directly or indirectly, with any victim, witness or other person specified in the direction except in accordance with any conditions specified in the direction that the officer considers necessary.
Paragraph 159.2(a) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: custody is necessary to ensure the person’s attendance before a court martial or civil court to be dealt with according to law;

Section.Text: The Act is amended by adding the following after section 159.3:

If a military judge directs that a person be retained in custody, the military judge may also direct that the person abstain from communicating, directly or indirectly, with any victim, witness or other person specified in the direction, except in accordance with any conditions specified in the direction that the military judge considers necessary.
Section 159.7 of the Act is renumbered as subsection 159.7(1) and is amended by adding the following:

Subsection.MarginalNote: Consideration of victim’s safety and security
Subsection.Text: If the military judge directs that a person be released, with or without conditions, the military judge shall include in the direction a statement that he or she has considered the safety and security of every victim of the alleged offence.
The military judge shall, on request by a victim of the alleged offence, cause a copy of the direction to be given to the victim.

Section.Text: Sections 160 to 161.1 of the Act are replaced by the following:

In this Division, commanding officer, in respect of a person charged with having committed a service offence or a service infraction, means the commanding officer of the person and includes an officer who is empowered by regulations made by the Governor in Council to act as the commanding officer of the person.

Subsection.Text: Proceedings against a person who is alleged to have committed a service offence or a service infraction are commenced by the laying of a charge in accordance with regulations made by the Governor in Council.
If the person is retained in custody or released from custody with conditions, a charge must be laid as expeditiously as the circumstances permit.

After a person is charged with having committed a service offence, the charge must be referred, in accordance with regulations made by the Governor in Council, to the Director of Military Prosecutions.

After a person is charged with having committed a service infraction, the charge must be referred, in accordance with regulations made by the Governor in Council, to an officer who is a commanding officer in respect of the person.

Section.Text: The heading before section 162.1 and sections 162.1 to 164.2 of the Act are replaced by the following:
The following definitions apply in this Division.

Definition.Text: commanding officer, in respect of a person charged with having committed a service infraction, means an officer who is a commanding officer as defined in section 160. (commandant)
delegated officer means an officer to whom a commanding officer has, under section 162.94, delegated the power to conduct a summary hearing. (officier délégué)
scale of sanctions means the scale of sanctions set out in subsection 162.7. (échelle des sanctions)
superior commander means an officer of or above the rank of colonel, or any other officer appointed by the Chief of the Defence Staff as a superior commander. (commandant supérieur)

Section.Text: Service infractions may be dealt with only by summary hearing.
A service infraction is not an offence under this Act.

Subsection.Text: If a person has been tried in respect of an offence, the person may not be charged with having committed a service infraction arising from the same facts, regardless of whether the person was found guilty or not guilty of the offence by a court martial, by a civil court or by a court of a foreign state.

If a summary hearing has been conducted in respect of a service infraction that a person is alleged to have committed, the person may be charged, dealt with and tried in respect of an offence arising from the same facts, regardless of whether or not the person was found to have committed the service infraction.

No answer given or statement made by a person at their summary hearing may be used or be receivable against them in any disciplinary, criminal or civil proceeding, other than at a hearing or proceeding in respect of an allegation that they gave the answer or made the statement knowing it to be false.

Section.Text: The following sanctions may be imposed in respect of a service infraction, and each is a sanction less than every sanction preceding it:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: reduction in rank;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: severe reprimand;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: reprimand;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: deprivation of pay, and of any allowance prescribed in regulations made by the Governor in Council, for not more than 18 days; and

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: minor sanctions prescribed in regulations made by the Governor in Council.

Subsection.Text: The sanction of reduction in rank applies to officers above the rank of second lieutenant and to non-commissioned members above the rank of private.
The sanction of reduction in rank is not to involve

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: reduction to a rank lower than that to which the person who has committed a service infraction can be reduced under regulations; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: in the case of a commissioned officer, reduction to a rank lower than commissioned rank.

Section.Text: The imposition of sanctions is intended to achieve one or more of the following objectives:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: to promote a habit of obedience to lawful commands and orders;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: to maintain public trust in the Canadian Forces as a disciplined armed force;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: to denounce indisciplined conduct;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: to deter persons from committing service infractions;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: to assist in rehabilitating persons who have committed service infractions;

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: to promote a sense of responsibility in persons who have committed service infractions.

Section.Text: Sanctions must be proportionate to the gravity of the service infraction and the degree of responsibility of the person who committed it.
Sanctions must be imposed in accordance with the following other principles:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: a sanction should be increased or reduced to account for any relevant aggravating or mitigating circumstances relating to the commission of the service infraction or the person who committed it, and aggravating circumstances include evidence establishing that

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: the person, in committing the service infraction, abused their rank or other position of trust or authority,

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: the service infraction was motivated by bias, prejudice or hate based on race, national or ethnic origin, language, colour, religion, sex, age, mental or physical disability, sexual orientation, or gender identity or expression, or on any other similar factor, or

Subparagraph.Label: (iii)
Subparagraph.Text: the commission of the service infraction resulted in harm to the conduct of a military operation or any military training;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: a sanction should be similar to sanctions imposed on persons for similar service infractions committed in similar circumstances; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: a sanction should be the least severe sanction required to maintain the discipline, efficiency and morale of the Canadian Forces.

Section.Text: In determining the sanction to be imposed against a person, a superior commander, commanding officer or delegated officer may take into account any indirect consequences of the finding that the person committed a service infraction, or of the sanction.

A commanding officer may, subject to regulations made by the Governor in Council and to the extent that the commanding officer considers appropriate, delegate his or her power to conduct a summary hearing to any officer under his or her command.
A commanding officer to whom a charge alleging the commission of a service infraction is referred under subsection 161.1(2) shall, taking into account the conditions set out in section 163,

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: conduct a summary hearing in respect of the charge;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: decide to not proceed with the charge if, in his or her opinion, it should not be proceeded with; or

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: refer the charge, subject to and in accordance with regulations made by the Governor in Council, to another commanding officer, a superior commander or a delegated officer.

Subsection.Text: A superior commander, commanding officer or delegated officer may conduct a hearing in respect of a charge alleging the commission of a service infraction if all of the following conditions are satisfied:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the person charged is an officer who is at least one rank below the rank of the superior commander, commanding officer or delegated officer, or is a non-commissioned member;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: having regard to the gravity of the facts that gave rise to the charge, the powers of the superior commander, commanding officer or delegated officer to impose a sanction are adequate;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: there are no reasonable grounds to believe that the person charged is unable on account of mental disorder to understand the nature, object or possible consequences of the proceedings; and

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: having regard to the interests of discipline, efficiency and morale of the Canadian Forces, it would be appropriate to conduct the hearing.

Subsection.Text: A superior commander, commanding officer or delegated officer may not conduct a hearing if he or she

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: carried out or directly supervised the investigation of the service infraction;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: issued a warrant under section 273.3 in relation to anything referred to in any of paragraphs 273.3(a) to (c) that relates to the service infraction; or

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: laid the charge or caused it to be laid.

ContinuedSectionSubsection.Text: However, he or she may conduct such a hearing if, having regard to all the circumstances, it is not practicable for any other superior commander, commanding officer or delegated officer to conduct the hearing.

Subsection.Text: A superior commander who finds on a balance of probabilities, at a summary hearing, that a person has committed one or more service infractions, may impose one or more of the sanctions referred to in section 162.7.

A commanding officer who finds on a balance of probabilities, at a summary hearing, that a person has committed one or more service infractions, may impose one or more of the sanctions referred to in paragraphs 162.7(c) to (e).
A delegated officer who finds on a balance of probabilities, at a summary hearing, that a person has committed one or more service infractions, may impose one or more of the following sanctions:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: a sanction referred to in paragraph 162.7(d) for not more than seven days;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: minor sanctions referred to in paragraph 162.7(e).

Section.Text: A superior commander, commanding officer or delegated officer to whom a charge is referred under paragraph 162.95(c) or under this section shall, taking into account the conditions set out in section 163,

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: conduct a summary hearing in respect of the charge;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: decide not to proceed with the charge if, in his or her opinion, it should not be proceeded with; or

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: refer the charge, subject to and in accordance with regulations made by the Governor in Council, to a superior commander, commanding officer or delegated officer.

Section.MarginalNote: Subsequent summary hearing proceedings not precluded
Section.Text: A decision that a charge should not be proceeded with by summary hearing does not preclude, subject to section 163.4, proceeding with the charge by summary hearing at any subsequent time.

A summary hearing in respect of a charge alleging the commission of a service infraction may not be conducted unless it commences within six months after the day on which the service infraction is alleged to have been committed.

Every person alleged to have committed a service infraction may be charged under the Code of Service Discipline, regardless of whether the alleged service infraction was committed in Canada or outside Canada, and the summary hearing in respect of that charge may be conducted in Canada or outside Canada.

Section.MarginalNote: Chief of the Defence Staff and other military authorities

Subsection.Text: The review authorities in respect of a finding that a person has committed a service infraction and in respect of a sanction imposed by an officer who conducted a summary hearing are the Chief of the Defence Staff and any other military authorities that are prescribed by the Governor in Council in regulations.

A review authority in respect of a finding that a person has committed a service infraction and in respect of a sanction imposed by an officer who conducted a summary hearing may act on its own initiative or on application, made in accordance with regulations made by the Governor in Council, of the person found to have committed the service infraction.
A review authority may quash a finding, by an officer who conducted a summary hearing, that a person has committed a service infraction.

If a finding that a person has committed a service infraction is quashed and no other such finding was made at the summary hearing, every sanction imposed as a result of the quashed finding is also quashed and a new summary hearing may be held in relation to the service infraction as if no previous summary hearing had been held.

In the case where more than one finding has been made that a person has committed a service infraction and a review authority quashes one or more but not all of them, if a sanction imposed is in excess of any that may be imposed in respect of the remaining findings or is, in the opinion of the review authority, unduly severe, the review authority shall substitute for that sanction any new sanction or sanctions that it considers appropriate.

Section.MarginalNote: Substitution of invalid or unsubstantiated findings

Subsection.Text: A review authority may substitute a new finding for any finding that a person has committed a service infraction that was invalidly made or that cannot be supported by the evidence if the new finding could validly have been made on the charge to which the new finding relates and it appears to the review authority that the officer who conducted the summary hearing was satisfied of the facts that establish the service infraction specified or involved in the new finding.

If a new finding is substituted and a sanction imposed in respect of the original finding is in excess of a sanction that may be imposed in respect of the new finding or is, in the opinion of the review authority, unduly severe, the review authority shall substitute for that sanction any new sanction or sanctions that it considers appropriate.
A review authority may substitute for any invalid sanction imposed by an officer who conducted a summary hearing any new sanction or sanctions that it considers appropriate.
If a new sanction is substituted, the new sanction may not be higher in the scale of sanctions than that other sanction.

Heading.TitleText: Commutation, Mitigation and Remission of Sanctions

Section.MarginalNote: Authority to commute, mitigate or remit sanctions

Subsection.Text: A review authority may commute, mitigate or remit any or all of the sanctions imposed by an officer who conducted a summary hearing.
The following definitions apply in subsection (1).

Definition.Text: commute means to replace a sanction with another sanction that is lower in the scale of sanctions. (commuer)
mitigate means to impose a lesser amount of the same sanction. (mitiger)
remit means to exempt a person from the requirement to undergo the whole or any part of a sanction. (remettre)

Section.Text: Section 165.13 of the Act is replaced by the following:

If the Director of Military Prosecutions decides that a charge should not be proceeded with by a trial by court martial, he or she shall communicate the decision and the reasons for it, in writing, to the officer or non-commissioned member who referred the charge to him or her, and to the commanding officer of the accused person.
Section 180 of the Act and the heading before it are replaced by the following:

Heading.TitleText: Admission to Courts Martial and Certain Proceedings Before Military Judges

Subsection.Text: Unless this Act provides otherwise, court martial proceedings and proceedings before a military judge under any of sections 147.6, 159, 187 and 248.81 must be public and, to the extent that accommodation permits, the public must be admitted to the proceedings.

A military judge or, if a court martial has been convened, the military judge assigned to preside at the court martial may, on application of the prosecutor or a witness or on the military judges’s own motion, order that the public be excluded during the whole or any part of the proceedings or order that the witness testify behind a screen or other device that would allow the witness not to be seen by members of the public, if the military judge considers that it is necessary in the interests of public safety, public morals, the maintenance of order or the proper administration of military justice, or to prevent injury to international relations, national defence or national security.
In determining whether making the order is in the interest of the proper administration of military justice, the military judge shall consider

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: society’s interest in encouraging the reporting of service offences and the participation of victims and witnesses in the military justice process;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the safeguarding of the interests of witnesses under the age of 18 years in all proceedings;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the ability of any witness, if the order were not made, to give a full and candid account of the acts complained of;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: whether any witness needs the order for their security or to protect them from intimidation or retaliation;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: the protection of military justice system participants who are involved in the proceedings;

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: whether effective alternatives to the making of the order are available in the circumstances;

Paragraph.Label: (g)
Paragraph.Text: the salutary and deleterious effects of the order; and

Paragraph.Label: (h)
Paragraph.Text: any other factor that the military judge considers relevant.

Subsection.Text: No adverse inference may be drawn from the fact that an order is, or is not, made under this section.

If a person is charged with an offence punishable under section 130 that is an offence under section 151, 152, 153, 153.1 or 155, subsection 160(2) or (3) or section 163.1, 170, 171, 171.1, 172, 172.1, 172.2, 173, 271, 272, 273, 279.01, 279.011, 279.02, 279.03, 286.1, 286.2 or 286.3 of the Criminal Code and the prosecutor or the person applies for an order under subsection (2), the military judge shall, if no such order is made, state, by reference to the circumstances of the case, the reasons for not making an order.
Witnesses are not to be admitted to the proceedings except when under examination or by leave of the military judge.
For the purpose of any deliberation, the military judge may cause the place where the proceedings are being held to be cleared.

Section.Text: For the purposes of sections 180.02 to 180.08, record means any form of record that contains personal information for which there is a reasonable expectation of privacy and includes medical, psychiatric, therapeutic, counselling, education, employment, child welfare, adoption and social services records, personal journals and diaries, and records containing personal information the production or disclosure of which is protected by any other Act of Parliament or of a provincial legislature, but does not include records made by persons responsible for the investigation or prosecution of the service offence.

Subsection.Text: Except in accordance with sections 180.03 to 180.08, no record relating to a complainant or a witness shall be produced to an accused person in any proceedings in respect of any of the following offences or in any proceedings in respect of two or more offences at least one of which is any of the following offences:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: an offence punishable under section 130 that is an offence under section 151, 152, 153, 153.1, 155, 160, 170, 171, 172, 173, 210, 211, 213, 271, 272, 273, 279.01, 279.011, 279.02, 279.03, 286.1, 286.2 or 286.3 of the Criminal Code;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: any offence under the Criminal Code, as it read at any time before the day on which this paragraph comes into force, if the conduct alleged involved a violation of the complainant’s sexual integrity and would be an offence referred to in paragraph (a) if it had occurred on or after that day.

Subsection.Text: Section 180.01, this section and sections 180.03 to 180.08 and 303 apply in respect of a record relating to the complainant or a witness that is in the possession or control of any person, including the prosecutor in the proceedings, unless, in the case of a record in the possession or control of the prosecutor, the complainant or witness, as the case may be, has expressly waived the application of those sections.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Duty of prosecutor to give notice
Subsection.Text: In the case of a record in respect of which this section applies that is in the possession or control of the prosecutor, the prosecutor shall notify the accused person that the record is in the prosecutor’s possession or control, but, in doing so, the prosecutor shall not disclose the record’s contents.

An accused person who seeks the production of a record may make an application for its production to a military judge or, if the court martial has been convened, to the military judge assigned to preside at the court martial.
The application must be made in writing and set out

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: particulars identifying the record that the accused person seeks to have produced and the name of the person who has possession or control of the record; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the grounds on which the accused person relies to establish that the record is likely relevant to an issue at trial or to the competence of a witness to testify.

Subsection.Text: Any one or more of the following assertions by the accused person are not sufficient on their own to establish that the record is likely relevant to an issue at trial or to the competence of a witness to testify:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the record exists;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the record relates to medical or psychiatric treatment, therapy or counselling that the complainant or witness has received or is receiving;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the record relates to the incident that is the subject matter of the proceedings;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: the record may disclose a prior inconsistent statement of the complainant or witness;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: the record may relate to the credibility of the complainant or witness;

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: the record may relate to the reliability of the testimony of the complainant or witness merely because the complainant or witness has received or is receiving psychiatric treatment, therapy or counselling;

Paragraph.Label: (g)
Paragraph.Text: the record may reveal allegations of sexual abuse of the complainant by a person other than the accused person;

Paragraph.Label: (h)
Paragraph.Text: the record relates to the sexual activity of the complainant with any person, including the accused person;

Paragraph.Label: (i)
Paragraph.Text: the record relates to the presence or absence of a recent complaint;

Paragraph.Label: (j)
Paragraph.Text: the record relates to the complainant’s sexual reputation;

Paragraph.Label: (k)
Paragraph.Text: the record was made close in time to the complaint or to the activity that forms the subject matter of the charge against the accused person.

Subsection.Text: The accused person shall serve the application on the prosecutor, on the person who has possession or control of the record, on the complainant or witness, as the case may be, and on any other person to whom, to the knowledge of the accused person, the record relates, at least 14 days before the hearing referred to in subsection 180.04(1) begins or any shorter interval that the military judge may allow in the interests of military justice.
The military judge may, at any time, order that the application be served on any person to whom he or she considers the record may relate.
The military judge shall hold a hearing in private to determine whether to order the person who has possession or control of the record to produce it to the military judge for review.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Persons who may appear at hearing
Subsection.Text: The person who has possession or control of the record, the complainant or witness, as the case may be, and any other person to whom the record relates may appear and make submissions at the hearing, but they are not compellable as witnesses at the hearing.
The military judge shall, as soon as feasible, inform any person referred to in subsection (2) who participates in the hearing of their right to be represented by counsel.
No order for costs may be made against a person referred to in subsection (2) in respect of their participation in the hearing.

Section.MarginalNote: Order to produce record for review

Subsection.Text: The military judge may order the person who has possession or control of the record to produce the record or a part of the record to the military judge for review if, after the hearing, the military judge is satisfied that

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the application was made in accordance with subsections 180.03(2) to (5);

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the accused person has established that the record is likely relevant to an issue at trial or to the competence of a witness to testify; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the production of the record is necessary in the interests of military justice.

Subsection.Text: In determining whether to make the order, the military judge shall consider the salutary and deleterious effects of the determination on the accused person’s right to make a full answer and defence and on the right to privacy, personal security and equality of the complainant or witness, as the case may be, and of any other person to whom the record relates. In particular, the military judge shall take the following factors into account:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the extent to which the record is necessary for the accused person to make a full answer and defence;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the probative value of the record;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the nature and extent of the reasonable expectation of privacy with respect to the record;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: whether production of the record is based on a discriminatory belief or bias;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: the potential prejudice to the personal dignity and right to privacy of any person to whom the record relates;

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: society’s interest in encouraging the reporting of sexual offences;

Paragraph.Label: (g)
Paragraph.Text: society’s interest in encouraging the obtaining of treatment by complainants of sexual offences; and

Paragraph.Label: (h)
Paragraph.Text: the effect of the determination on the integrity of the trial process.

Section.MarginalNote: Review of record by military judge

Subsection.Text: If the military judge has ordered the production of the record or a part of the record for review, the military judge shall review it in the absence of the parties to determine whether the record or the part of the record should be produced to the accused person.
The military judge may hold a hearing in private if he or she considers that it will assist in making the determination.
Subsections 180.04(2) to (4) apply in the case of a hearing held under subsection (2).

If the military judge is satisfied that the record or a part of the record is likely relevant to an issue at trial or to the competence of a witness to testify and that its production is necessary in the interests of military justice, the military judge may order that the record or the part of the record, as the case may be, be produced to the accused person, subject to any conditions that may be imposed under subsection (3).

In determining whether to make the order, the military judge shall consider the salutary and deleterious effects of the determination on the accused person’s right to make a full answer and defence and on the right to privacy, personal security and equality of the complainant or witness, as the case may be, and of any other person to whom the record relates and, in particular, the military judge shall take the factors specified in paragraphs 180.05(2)(a) to (h) into account.

The military judge who orders the production of the record or a part of the record to the accused person, may impose conditions on the production to protect the interests of military justice and, to the greatest extent possible, the privacy, personal security and equality interests of the complainant or witness, as the case may be, and of any other person to whom the record relates, including the following conditions:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the record is to be edited as directed by the military judge;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: a copy of the record, rather than the original, is to be produced;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the accused person and counsel for the accused person are not to disclose the contents of the record to any other person, except with the approval of the military judge;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: the record is to be viewed only at a location specified by the military judge;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: no copies of the record are to be made or only the number of copies specified by the military judge may be made; and

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: information regarding any person named in the record, such as their address, telephone number and place of employment, is to be severed from the record.

Subsection.Text: If the military judge orders the production of the record or a part of the record to the accused person, the military judge shall direct that a copy of the record or the part of the record, as the case may be, be provided to the prosecutor, unless the military judge determines that it is not in the interests of military justice to do so.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Record not used in other proceedings
Subsection.Text: The record or the part of the record that is produced to the accused person under an order made under subsection (1) must not be used in any other disciplinary, criminal, civil or administrative proceedings.

If the military judge refuses to order the production of the record or a part of the record to the accused person, the record or the part of the record, as the case may be, must, unless the military judge orders otherwise, be kept by the military judge in a sealed package until the later of the expiry of the time for any appeal and the completion of any appeal in the proceedings against the accused person, at which time the record or the part of the record must be returned to the person lawfully entitled to possession or control of it.

Section.Text: The military judge shall provide reasons in writing for ordering or not ordering the production of the record or a part of the record under subsection 180.05(1) or 180.07(1).
The Act is amended by adding the following after section 183:

Section.MarginalNote: Support person — witnesses under 18 or with disability

Subsection.Text: In proceedings against an accused person in respect of a service offence, a military judge or, if the court martial has been convened, the military judge assigned to preside at the court martial shall, on application of the prosecutor in respect of a witness who is under the age of 18 years or who has a mental or physical disability, or on application of such a witness, order that a support person of the witness’s choice be permitted to be present and to be close to the witness while the witness testifies, unless the military judge is of the opinion that the order would interfere with the proper administration of military justice.

In proceedings against an accused person in respect of a service offence, a military judge or, if the court martial has been convened, the military judge assigned to preside at the court martial may, on application of the prosecutor in respect of a witness, or on application of a witness, order that a support person of the witness’s choice be permitted to be present and to be close to the witness while the witness testifies, if the military judge is of the opinion that the order would facilitate the giving of a full and candid account by the witness of the acts complained of or would otherwise be in the interest of the proper administration of military justice.
In determining whether to make an order under subsection (2), the military judge shall consider

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the witness’s age;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the witness’s mental or physical disabilities, if any;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the nature of the offence;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: the nature of any relationship between the witness and the accused person;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: whether the witness needs the order for their security or to protect them from intimidation or retaliation;

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: society’s interest in encouraging the reporting of service offences and the participation of victims and witnesses in the military justice process; and

Paragraph.Label: (g)
Paragraph.Text: any other factor that the military judge considers relevant.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Witness not to be support person
Subsection.Text: The military judge shall not permit a witness to be a support person unless the military judge is of the opinion that doing so is necessary for the proper administration of military justice.
The military judge may order that the support person and the witness not communicate with each other while the witness testifies.
No adverse inference may be drawn from the fact that an order is, or is not, made under subsection (2).

Section.MarginalNote: Testimony outside courtroom — witnesses under 18 or with disability

Subsection.Text: In proceedings against an accused person in respect of a service offence, a military judge or, if the court martial has been convened, the military judge assigned to preside at the court martial shall, on application of the prosecutor in respect of a witness who is under the age of 18 years or who is able to communicate evidence but may have difficulty doing so by reason of a mental or physical disability, or on application of such a witness, order that the witness testify outside the courtroom or behind a screen or other device that would allow the witness not to see the accused person, unless the military judge is of the opinion that the order would interfere with the proper administration of military justice.

In proceedings against an accused person in respect of a service offence, a military judge or, if the court martial has been convened, the military judge assigned to preside at the court martial may, on application of the prosecutor in respect of a witness, or on application of a witness, order that the witness testify outside the courtroom or behind a screen or other device that would allow the witness not to see the accused person, if the military judge is of the opinion that the order would facilitate the giving of a full and candid account by the witness of the acts complained of or would otherwise be in the interest of the proper administration of military justice.
In determining whether to make an order under subsection (2), the military judge shall consider

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the witness’s age;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the witness’s mental or physical disabilities, if any;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the nature of the offence;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: the nature of any relationship between the witness and the accused person;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: whether the witness needs the order for their security or to protect them from intimidation or retaliation;

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: whether the order is needed to protect the identity of a peace officer, as defined in section 2 of the Criminal Code, who has acted, is acting or will be acting in an undercover capacity or of a person who has acted, is acting or will be acting covertly under the direction of a peace officer as defined in that section;

Paragraph.Label: (g)
Paragraph.Text: whether the order is needed to protect the witness’s identity if they have had, have or will have responsibilities relating to national security or intelligence;

Paragraph.Label: (h)
Paragraph.Text: society’s interest in encouraging the reporting of service offences and the participation of victims and witnesses in the military justice process; and

Paragraph.Label: (i)
Paragraph.Text: any other factor that the military judge considers relevant.

Subsection.Text: If the military judge is of the opinion that it is necessary for a witness to testify in order to determine whether an order under subsection (2) should be made in respect of the witness, the military judge shall order that the witness testify in accordance with that subsection.

A witness shall not testify outside the courtroom in accordance with an order made under subsection (1) or (2) unless arrangements are made for the accused person, the military judge and, if a General Court Martial has been convened, its panel to watch the testimony of the witness by means of closed-circuit television or otherwise and the accused person is permitted to communicate with counsel while watching the testimony.
No adverse inference may be drawn from the fact that an order is, or is not, made under subsection (2).

Section.MarginalNote: Accused not to cross-examine witnesses under 18

Subsection.Text: In proceedings against an accused person in respect of a service offence, a military judge or, if the court martial has been convened, the military judge assigned to preside at the court martial shall, on application of the prosecutor in respect of a witness who is under the age of 18 years, or on application of such a witness, order that the accused person not personally cross-examine the witness, unless the military judge is of the opinion that the proper administration of military justice requires the accused person to personally conduct the cross-examination. If such an order is made, the military judge shall direct the Director of Defence Counsel Services to provide counsel to conduct the cross-examination.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Accused not to cross-examine complainant — certain offences
Subsection.Text: In proceedings against an accused person in respect of an offence punishable under section 130 that is an offence under section 264, 271, 272 or 273 of the Criminal Code, a military judge shall, on application of the prosecutor in respect of a witness who is a victim, or on application of such a witness, order that the accused person not personally cross-examine the witness, unless the military judge is of the opinion that the proper administration of military justice requires the accused person to personally conduct the cross-examination. If such an order is made, the military judge shall direct the Director of Defence Counsel Services to provide counsel to conduct the cross-examination.

In proceedings against an accused person in respect of a service offence, a military judge or, if the court martial has been convened, the military judge assigned to preside at the court martial may, on application of the prosecutor in respect of a witness who is not entitled to make an application under subsection (1) or (2), or on application of such a witness, order that the accused person not personally cross-examine the witness, if the military judge is of the opinion that the order would facilitate the giving of a full and candid account by the witness of the acts complained of or would otherwise be in the interest of the proper administration of military justice. If such an order is made, the military judge shall direct the Director of Defence Counsel Services to provide counsel to conduct the cross-examination.
In determining whether to make an order under subsection (3), the military judge shall consider

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the witness’s age;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the witness’s mental or physical disabilities, if any;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the nature of the offence;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: the nature of any relationship between the witness and the accused person;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: whether the witness needs the order for their security or to protect them from intimidation or retaliation;

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: society’s interest in encouraging the reporting of service offences and the participation of victims and witnesses in the military justice process; and

Paragraph.Label: (g)
Paragraph.Text: any other factor that the military judge considers relevant.

Subsection.Text: No adverse inference may be drawn from the fact that counsel is, or is not, provided under this section.

In proceedings against an accused person in respect of a service offence, a military judge or, if the court martial has been convened, the military judge assigned to preside at the court martial may, on application of the prosecutor in respect of a witness, or on application of a witness, make an order directing that any information that could identify the witness not be disclosed in the course of the proceedings, if the military judge is of the opinion that the order is in the interest of the proper administration of military justice.
The military judge may hold a hearing to determine whether the order should be made, and the hearing may be in private.
In determining whether to make the order, the military judge shall consider

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the right to a fair and public hearing;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the nature of the offence;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: whether the witness needs the order for their security or to protect them from intimidation or retaliation;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: whether the order is needed to protect the security of anyone known to the witness;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: whether the order is needed to protect the identity of a peace officer, as defined in section 2 of the Criminal Code, who has acted, is acting or will be acting in an undercover capacity, or of a person who has acted, is acting or will be acting covertly under the direction of a peace officer as defined in that section;

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: whether the order is needed to protect the witness’s identity if they have had, have or will have responsibilities relating to national security or intelligence;

Paragraph.Label: (g)
Paragraph.Text: society’s interest in encouraging the reporting of service offences and the participation of victims and witnesses in the military justice process;

Paragraph.Label: (h)
Paragraph.Text: the importance of the witness’s testimony to the case;

Paragraph.Label: (i)
Paragraph.Text: whether effective alternatives to the making of the order are available in the circumstances;

Paragraph.Label: (j)
Paragraph.Text: the salutary and deleterious effects of the order; and

Paragraph.Label: (k)
Paragraph.Text: any other factor that the military judge considers relevant.

Subsection.Text: No adverse inference may be drawn from the fact that an order is, or is not, made under this section.

Subject to subsection (2), a military judge or, if the court martial has been convened, the military judge assigned to preside at the court martial may make an order directing that any information that could identify a victim or a witness not be published in any document, or broadcast or transmitted in any way, if the proceedings are in respect of

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: any of the following offences:

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: any offence under the Criminal Code, as it read at any time before the day on which this subparagraph comes into force, if the conduct alleged would be an offence referred to in subparagraph (i) if it had occurred on or after that day; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: two or more offences being dealt with in the same proceeding, at least one of which is an offence referred to in paragraph (a).

Subsection.Text: In proceedings in respect of any offence referred to in subsection (1), the military judge shall

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: as soon as feasible, inform the victim and any witness under the age of 18 years of their right to make an application for the order; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: on application of the victim, the prosecutor or any such witness, make the order.

Subsection.Text: Subject to subsection (4), in proceedings in respect of a service offence other than an offence referred to in subsection (1), if the victim is under the age of 18 years, the military judge or, if the court martial has been convened, the military judge assigned to preside at the court martial may make an order directing that any information that could identify the victim not be published in any document or broadcast or transmitted in any way.

In proceedings in respect of a service offence other than an offence referred to in subsection (1), if the victim is under the age of 18 years, the military judge or, if the court martial has been convened, the military judge assigned to preside at the court martial shall

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: as soon as feasible, inform the victim of their right to make an application for the order; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: on application of the victim or the prosecutor, make the order.

Subsection.Text: In proceedings in respect of an offence punishable under section 130 that is an offence under section 163.1 of the Criminal Code, the military judge or, if the court martial has been convened, the military judge assigned to preside at the court martial shall make an order directing that any information that could identify a witness who is under the age of 18 years or any person who is the subject of any representation, written material or recording that constitutes child pornography, as defined in that section 163.1, not be published in any document or broadcast or transmitted in any way.

An order made under this section does not apply in respect of the disclosure of information if the disclosure is made in the course of the administration of military justice and it is made for a purpose other than to make the information known in the community.

Section.MarginalNote: Order restricting publication — victims and witnesses

Subsection.Text: Unless an order is made under section 183.5, on application of the prosecutor in respect of a victim or a witness, or on application of a victim or a witness, a military judge or, if the court martial has been convened, the military judge assigned to preside at the court martial may make an order directing that any information that could identify the victim or witness not be published in any document or broadcast or transmitted in any way, if the military judge is of the opinion that the order is in the interest of the proper administration of military justice.

On application of the prosecutor in respect of a military justice system participant who is involved in proceedings in respect of an offence referred to in subsection (3) or on application of the military justice system participant themself, a military judge or, if the court martial has been convened, the military judge assigned to preside at the court martial may make an order directing that any information that could identify the military justice system participant not be published in any document or broadcast or transmitted in any way, if the military judge is of the opinion that the order is in the interest of the proper administration of military justice.
For the purpose of subsection (2), an offence is any of the following:
